Summary: In T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (T-ALL), more than 50% of cases are associated with DNA alterations in NOTCH1 or in other genes that lead to enhanced activation of the NOTCH1 pathway. Constitutive expression of the NOTCH1-ΔE variant that lacks the extracellular domain leads to overactive NOTCH1 signaling. Overactive NOTCH1 signaling enforces T-cell differentiation and drives development of T-ALL–like disease. To test dimeric signaling in leukemogenesis, researchers transplanted cord blood hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells transduced with NOTCH1-ΔE or with the dimerization-defective NOTCH1-ΔE-R1984A, alone or with LMO2 or LMO2-TAL1-BMI1, into sublethally irradiated NSG mice. Only cells expressing NOTCH1-ΔE wild-type generated T-ALL–like leukemias, and the dimerization-defective NOTCH1-ΔE-R1984A mutant did not. Clinically morbid animals displayed hepatosplenomegaly, enlarged lymph nodes and thymus masses, hypercellular bone marrow with extensive infiltration of leukemic blasts, and circulating leukemia cells. NOTCH1 dimerization produced a gene signature that included HES4. HES4 conferred a proliferative advantage in hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells and in Notch dimerization–dependent xenografts. HES4 enforced Δ133p53 expression and blocked proapoptotic p53 targets. HES4 induced BCL2L1 and BCL-XL. Subsets of primary T-ALL with Notch dimerization–dependent signatures were identified, and those subsets correlated indirectly with proapoptotic genes and directly with markers of poor clinical outcome in primary T-ALL. The findings highlight NOTCH1 dimeric signaling as crucial for T-cell leukemogenesis and maintenance and suggest targeting NOTCH1 dimerization or its downstream effectors.
References: Tamiro, F., Padovano, C., De Santis, E., Di Iasio, S., Sansico, D. F., Canistro, V., Colucci, M., Di Nunzio, C., Bruno, G., Doshi, K., Totaro, A., Gu, E., Santodirocco, M., Weng, A. P., & Giambra, V. (2025). NOTCH1 dimeric signaling is essential for T-cell leukemogenesis and leukemia maintenance. Blood, 145(24), 2887-2902. https://doi.org/10.1182/blood.2024027020
